title: Charmed Life — 4 (Fragment 2)
sourceText:
“Make up your mind,” Euphemia said at last. “My breakfast’s waiting for me too, you know.”

“Isn’t there any marmalade?” said Gwendolen.

Euphemia and Mary looked at one another. “Julia and Roger are not allowed marmalade,” Mary said.

“Nobody forbade me to have it,” said Gwendolen. “Get me some marmalade at once.”

Mary went to a speaking tube by the lift and, after much rumbling and another clank, a pot of marmalade arrived. Mary brought it and put it in front of Gwendolen.

“Thank you,” Cat said fervently. He felt as strongly about it as Gwendolen—more, in fact, because he hated cocoa.

“Oh, no trouble, I’m sure!” Mary said, in what was certainly a sarcastic way, and the two maids went out.

For a while, nobody said anything. Then Roger said to Cat, “Pass the marmalade, please.”

“You’re not supposed to have it,” said Gwendolen, whose temper had not improved.

“Nobody will know if I use one of your knives,” Roger said placidly.

Cat passed him the marmalade and his knife too. “Why aren’t you allowed it?”

Julia and Roger looked at one another in a mild, secretive way. “We’re too fat,” Julia said, calmly taking the knife and the marmalade after Roger had done with them. Cat was not surprised when he saw how much marmalade they had managed to pile on their bread. Marmalade stood on both slices like a sticky brown cliff.

Gwendolen looked at them with disgust, and then, rather complacently, down at her trim linen dress. The contrast was certainly striking. “Your father is such a handsome man,” she said. “It must be such a disappointment to him that you’re both pudgy and plain, like your mother.”

The two children looked at her placidly over their cliffs of marmalade. “Oh, I wouldn’t know,” said Roger.

“Pudgy is comfortable,” said Julia. “It must be a nuisance to look like a china doll, the way you do.”

Gwendolen’s blue eyes glared. She made a small sign under the edge of the table. The bread and thick marmalade whisked itself from Julia’s hands and slapped itself on Julia’s face, marmalade side inward. Julia gasped a little. “How dare you insult me!” said Gwendolen.

Julia peeled the bread slowly off her face and then fumbled out a handkerchief. Cat supposed she was going to wipe her face. But she let the marmalade stay where it was, trundling in blobs down her plump cheeks, and simply tied a knot in the handkerchief. She pulled the knot slowly tight, looking meaningly at Gwendolen while she did so. With the final pull, the half-full jug of cocoa shot steaming into the air. It hovered for a second, and then shot sideways to hang just above Gwendolen’s head. Then it began to joggle itself into tipping position.

“Stop it!” gasped Gwendolen. She put up a hand to ward the jug off. The jug dodged her and went on tipping. Gwendolen made another sign and gasped out strange words. The jug took not the slightest notice. It went on tipping until cocoa was brimming in the very end of its spout. Gwendolen tried to lean out sideways away from it. The jug simply joggled along in the air until it was hanging over her head again.

words.definition: A preserve made from citrus fruit, especially bitter oranges, with sugar and water.
words.example: He spread marmalade on his toast each morning.
words.exampleText: Isn’t there any marmalade?” said Gwendolen.

words.definition: In a way that shows strong, sincere and intense feeling.
words.example: She fervently hoped for his success.
words.exampleText: “Thank you,” Cat said fervently.

words.definition: Using irony to mock or convey contempt.
words.example: He made a sarcastic remark about her cooking.
words.exampleText: ...in what was certainly a sarcastic way.

words.definition: In a calm and peaceful manner, without disturbance or agitation.
words.example: He smiled placidly despite the chaos around him.
words.exampleText: Roger said placidly.

words.definition: A strong feeling of dislike or disapproval.
words.example: She looked at the mess with disgust.
words.exampleText: Gwendolen looked at them with disgust.

words.definition: In a self-satisfied, often unaware of possible dangers or defects, manner.
words.example: He nodded complacently after hearing the praise.
words.exampleText: Gwendolen looked at them complacently.

words.definition: Attracting attention by being unusual, extreme, or prominent.
words.example: Her striking appearance caught everyone's eye.
words.exampleText: The contrast was certainly striking.

words.definition: Slightly fat or chubby in a way that is often considered cute or endearing.
words.example: The baby had pudgy cheeks.
words.exampleText: We’re too fat,” Julia said; later described as pudgy.

words.definition: Attractive; pleasing to the eye (often used of women).
words.example: A comely young woman entered the room.
words.exampleText: (In reference to handsome and plain appearances contrasted)

words.definition: A person's state of mind in terms of being angry or calm; disposition.
words.example: She has a fiery temper.
words.exampleText: Gwendolen, whose temper had not improved.

words.definition: The feeling that a person or thing is worthless or beneath consideration.
words.example: He showed contempt for the rules.
words.exampleText: (Implied in sarcastic remarks and attitude.)

words.definition: To speak to or treat with disrespect or scornful abuse.
words.example: He insulted her intelligence.
words.exampleText: “How dare you insult me!” said Gwendolen.

words.definition: To handle clumsily or to do something awkwardly, especially when searching or trying to hold something.
words.example: He fumbled for the light switch in the dark.
words.exampleText: Julia peeled the bread slowly off her face and then fumbled out a handkerchief.

words.definition: In a way that expresses a significant or serious meaning.
words.example: He looked at her meaningly across the table.
words.exampleText: She pulled the knot slowly tight, looking meaningly at Gwendolen.

words.definition: To move or shake slightly and repeatedly.
words.example: The cart joggled along the uneven road.
words.exampleText: The jug began to joggle itself into tipping position.

words.definition: To prevent or repel something undesirable or harmful.
words.example: She held up her hand to ward off the blow.
words.exampleText: She put up a hand to ward the jug off.

words.definition: Filled completely or almost overflowing.
words.example: A cup brimming with tea.
words.exampleText: Cocoa was brimming in the very end of its spout.

words.definition: To hold on tightly to something or someone.
words.example: The child clung to her mother’s hand.
words.exampleText: (Implied by marmalade standing stiff like a cliff.)

words.definition: Showing or expressing contempt or disdain.
words.example: He gave a contemptuous laugh.
words.exampleText: (Related to sarcastic tone and insulting behaviour.)

words.definition: Extremely complex and difficult to follow.
words.example: The narrative was so convoluted that I lost track.
words.exampleText: (Implied in the complex social interactions.)

words.definition: An expression of disapproval or disappointment.
words.example: She cast him a look of reproach.
words.exampleText: (Implied in the disapproving remarks.)

words.definition: To tease or laugh at in a scornful or contemptuous manner.
words.example: They mocked his accent.
words.exampleText: (Sarcastic remarks mock others.)

words.definition: Used in ordinary or familiar conversation; not formal or literary.
words.example: They spoke in a colloquial manner.
words.exampleText: (Implied conversational narrative style.)

words.definition: A natural combination of words that are often used together.
words.example: Make a collocation dictionary.
words.exampleText: (Implied in multi-word expressions like 'make up your mind'.)

words.definition: A spoken or written account of connected events; a story.
words.example: The narrative was compelling and vivid.
words.exampleText: (Refers to the descriptive story fragment.)

words.definition: The action or feeling of expressing an unfavorable opinion.
words.example: She frowned in disapproval.
words.exampleText: (Implied in the social interactions.)
